trajectory_summary: This is not a standard setter-to-prospect call. The 'customer' is almost certainly an Ion internal contact or employee who received a misdirected outreach from the rep. The rep was price-shopping across multiple companies without knowing which one they'd reached, and the Ion contact resolved the confusion by routing them back to Alex. No qualification, no pitch, no appointment — the call ended in under three minutes with zero setter activity.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is listed as 'unknown,' which is appropriate. This call should be tagged as 'wrong party / internal misdial' — it is not a prospect call and should not be counted in setter performance metrics. Counting it as a missed opportunity would misrepresent the rep's pipeline activity.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Call prep and lead-source discipline before dialing
primary_coaching_focus.quote: I don't know. I've just been past a wide net trying to get this ballpark close of what the cost might be.
primary_coaching_focus.why: The rep's own words reveal they did not know who they were calling, why they were calling, or which company they'd reached. This is a pre-call discipline failure, not an in-call technique failure. The manager's first question in the 1-on-1 should be: how did this call enter the queue, and what does the rep's call-prep process look like before they dial? The answer determines whether this is a conduct issue or a training issue.

key_moments.quote: Hey. This is Carter calling from Ion. How are you?
key_moments.stewart_read: Carter's intro names the company but provides no context for why he's calling — no reference to a form, a Tesla referral, or any lead source. On a legitimate prospect call, this would be a legitimacy gap. Here it compounds the confusion because the 'customer' is actually an Ion contact trying to figure out why Ion is calling them.

key_moments.quote: I don't know. I've just been past a wide net trying to get this ballpark close of what the cost might be.
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep is not working a setter script at all — they are personally price-shopping solar across multiple companies. This is a fundamental role confusion: the rep is behaving as a consumer, not a setter. Whether this is a personal inquiry that accidentally routed through the Ion dialer, or a rep who has lost track of their call queue, the behavior is outside any setter framework.

key_moments.quote: I'm not sure. I've been texting people. I don't know if it's your company or a different one. No.
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep cannot identify the lead source or even confirm which company they've been contacting. On a legitimate call, this would be a critical legitimacy failure — the customer's first trust question ('how did you get my number / who are you?') is answered with confusion. The codex requires honest specificity; the rep delivers honest uncertainty, which is worse.

key_moments.quote: He'll probably just set you up an appointment to talk to one of our specialists who will go over the pricing for batteries.
key_moments.stewart_read: The Ion contact mentions 'pricing for batteries' — this is the only product signal in the call. The rep does not engage with it at all, responding only with 'Okay.' On a real prospect call, a customer expressing interest in battery pricing would be a direct cross-sell signal (Tesla Powerwall) and a qualification anchor. Here the rep has already been identified as a misdial, so engagement would be inappropriate — but the manager should note that the rep showed zero instinct to engage even when a product signal was handed to them.
